text: Upon initial entrance into the abdomen, the surgeon observes the right-sided spleen, midline liver, right-sided stomach, and gallbladder.
The surgeon observes extensive peritoneal attachments to surrounding structures, with Ladd's bands visible between the loops of small bowel and at the base of the gallbladder.

The video shows engorged and dilated large vessels on the right side of the screen. Enlarged loops of small bowel are visible in the left upper quadrant, with some small bowel diving posterior and inferior to the mesentery.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to perform extensive adhesiolysis, carefully reducing peritoneal attachments to mobilize the duodenum and small bowel, and to explore the base of the liver.

The surgeon examines the inferior wall of the gallbladder, revealing a pre-duodenal portal vein in the middle of the screen. The duodenum is visible coursing posterior to the vein and extending into the left upper quadrant.
The surgical view shows a right-sided stomach and spleen, a midline liver, and the gallbladder positioned on the right side of the screen. The anatomy is noted to be abnormal.

The surgeon dissects and removes peritoneal attachments and Ladd's bands, revealing the cause of the patient's intermittent obstruction due to small bowel kinking. Engorged vessels are visible, likely related to the kinking and pre-duodenal portal vein. As the dissection progresses, the duodenum and small bowel are straightened into a non-obstructive anatomical position, with the portal vein becoming more apparent.
Following the reduction of peritoneal attachments, the video shows extensive vascular congestion in the duodenum, likely caused by previous kinking before adhesiolysis.

The still frame shows the portal vein on the left, engorged vessels on the right, and the liver at the top of the screen. The small bowel is being manipulated down towards the cecum, revealing LADS bands attaching the small bowel to the cecum, which are reduced to further expose the appendix for the final portion of the procedure.
The appendix is identified in the right lower quadrant, appearing non-inflamed.
The appendix is extracorporealized and suture ligation is performed. The surgeon uses electrocautery to seal the opening of the appendix, concluding the procedure.
